title: Bad Parents

jokes.text: David resists coming into the room, arguing Johnny could have told him from where he was standing
jokes.setup: Johnny repeatedly asks David to come into the room to speak face to face
jokes.punchline: David points out they're in a motel — they're basically always face to face, in the same room
jokes.explanation: Efficiently establishes the cramped motel situation while revealing David's passive-aggressive personality. The logic is airtight and character-specific — David would absolutely weaponize spatial awareness to avoid compliance. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: David, we need to talk face to face.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: We live in a room the size of a walk-in closet. I can literally smell what you had for breakfast. I don't think 'face to face' is going to change the dynamic here.

jokes.text: Johnny tells David to move his clothes out of the closet because David has t-shirts on hangers
jokes.setup: Johnny asks David to move his clothes out of the closet
jokes.punchline: The reveal that David has t-shirts on hangers, leaving no room for anyone else — the absurdity of his fastidiousness in a motel room
jokes.explanation: Subtle but effective character comedy. David hanging t-shirts in a motel tells you everything about his personality immediately. Works as a visual detail that grounds the absurdity of the Roses' situation.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, if my clothes are taking up too much space, perhaps you should consider wearing them.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I'm not wearing your clothes, David.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Then I suppose you'll have to move out.

jokes.text: David suggests putting clothes 'over the t-shirts then' as a solution
jokes.setup: Johnny complains there's no room in the closet
jokes.punchline: David's solution is to just pile more clothes on top — completely missing the point with breezy confidence
jokes.explanation: Quick and economical. David's obliviousness wrapped in total confidence is his signature move, and this is a clean early example of it.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Do I have to put my middle name?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Yes, Alexis, it's a legal document.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: But like, do they really need to know that it's Rose?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Your middle name is Rose. That's perfectly lovely.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I know, but it makes me sound like I'm a person.

jokes.text: Moira describing Alexis as 'our only delinquent' while handing her community service papers
jokes.setup: Moira asks Alexis to fill out community service paperwork
jokes.punchline: 'As our only delinquent, I hope it's not asking too much' — delivered with total casualness, as if this is a perfectly ordinary family distinction
jokes.explanation: The phrase 'our only delinquent' is a perfect Moira construction — backhanded, theatrical, self-centered. It implies pride in categorizing her children. Strong character integration; only Moira says something this precisely cutting while thinking she's being reasonable.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I know your middle name.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh really? What is it?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, I... it's on the tip of my tongue.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yeah, that's what I thought.

jokes.text: Moira does not know Alexis's middle name — her own daughter
jokes.setup: Moira tells Alexis to fill in her middle name on the form
jokes.punchline: Alexis's question reveals Moira does not actually know it, confirmed when Moira can't produce it and fumbles with 'something rose'
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's first big comedic revelation and it lands hard because it escalates perfectly. Moira's imperious 'Of course I do — I blessed you with it' followed immediately by total inability to produce the name is a textbook misdirection. The structure is tight: confident claim → challenge → slow collapse → 'something rose.' Deeply character-specific; only a Rose parent would both not know and be incapable of admitting it. Strong rewatch value because in hindsight every beat telegraphs the reveal.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Your middle name is... Rose?

jokes.text: David: 'Wait. Alexis has a middle name? Why is this the first time hearing of this? Do... do I have a middle name?'
jokes.setup: The revelation that Moira doesn't know Alexis's middle name
jokes.punchline: David enters the room for the first time specifically to ask if HE has a middle name — turning Alexis's problem into his own existential crisis
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's strongest early joke. It works on multiple levels simultaneously: it's a callback to David refusing to enter the room earlier (now he enters for THIS), it escalates the middle name revelation into a deeper parental neglect joke, and David making it about himself is perfect character integration. 'Now you come into the room' from Johnny is the ideal button. Quotable, rewatchable, and structurally elegant — the three-beat rhythm of his questions ('Alexis has a middle name... first time hearing... do I have one?') builds beautifully.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Wait, wait, wait. What are we talking about? Middle names? Why are we discussing middle names?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Oh God. Oh God, no. Do I have a middle name?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I don't know my middle name. Why don't I know my middle name?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Mom! MOM! What is my middle name?

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Terrible parents?! We sent them to the best boarding schools, we hired the best nannies... We did everything right.'
jokes.setup: Moira asks if they are terrible parents
jokes.punchline: Johnny's defense of their parenting is entirely outsourced — boarding schools and nannies — obliviously proving her point
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the ironic self-incrimination — Johnny's proud recitation of all the ways they paid others to raise their children is genuinely funny because he doesn't hear himself. Well-structured dramatic irony.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Oh, there you are! I've been calling for you.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes, I heard you.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: And yet you took your time.

jokes.text: Ronnie to Alexis: 'So what's your deal? You're pretty. What's that like?'
jokes.setup: Ronnie approaches Alexis who is registering for community service
jokes.punchline: Ronnie's flat, philosophical inquiry about what it's like to be pretty — completely sincere and utterly unexpected
jokes.explanation: An extremely economical, original character introduction for Ronnie. The question 'What's that like?' is asked with genuine curiosity and zero malice, which makes it funnier than any snark would be. It's a perfect Schitt's Creek joke — warm, odd, specific. High quotability.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We've been wonderful parents. We let them make their own choices, we respected their independence, we never imposed our values on them...

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: We gave them space to grow, we trusted them implicitly, and we were always available if they needed us—which they never did, because we made it very clear we were busy.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Mm-hmm, clearly, as I register for community service.'
jokes.setup: Ronnie observes that doors open for pretty people
jokes.punchline: Alexis drily gestures to the community service registration table as evidence to the contrary
jokes.explanation: A crisp self-aware response from Alexis that shows more intelligence than the character usually gets credit for in early episodes. Good economy; the setup is built into the line.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So I got pulled over, and the officer asked me to step out of the car. And I was like, 'Do you know who I am?' And he was like, 'No.' So I told him. Twice. And then I asked him if he wanted to be in my music video because I was working on one at the time.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: And then he asked me to walk in a straight line, and I said, 'I don't do cardio.' So he did a breathalyzer test instead, and I blew a 0.15.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: But here's the thing—I had just come from a wine and chakra alignment session, so technically I was spiritually sober. And my lawyer says that doesn't hold up in court, but I really think it should.

jokes.text: Alexis explaining her DUI: 'I drove into the Prada Store on Rodeo Drive... it did look a lot like the entrance to a parking garage... And I was high at the time.'
jokes.setup: Ronnie asks what Alexis did to get community service
jokes.punchline: The three-beat escalation: Prada Store → looked like a parking garage → and I was high, each detail worse than the last
jokes.explanation: The escalation structure is well-executed. Each added detail deepens the joke — the Prada store is funny, the 'parking garage' excuse is funnier, and 'I was high at the time' delivers the actual punchline with perfect nonchalance. Alexis's breezy delivery of her own criminal history is core to her character.

dialogue.speaker: Ronnie
dialogue.line: You know what? Fine. Don't wear the vest. But when you're standing on the side of the road looking like you're about to either get married or do time, don't come crying to me.

jokes.text: Ronnie: 'Are you high right now?' Alexis: 'No.' Ronnie: 'Well, you might wanna be, 'cause this isn't exactly thrilling work.'
jokes.setup: Alexis says she was high when she drove into Prada
jokes.punchline: Ronnie non-judgmentally suggests sobriety might be a disadvantage for this particular job
jokes.explanation: Ronnie's deadpan practicality is her defining character trait and this line captures it perfectly. The inversion — a community service supervisor suggesting drugs might improve the experience — is a solid subversive moment.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Why would you buy clothes that are dry-clean only?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Because they're beautiful, Stevie. And if you throw them in the wash, the cashmere will shrink and cry itself to death.

jokes.text: Ronnie throws a garbage-picking stick at Alexis and says 'This isn't say yes to the dress, princess. Orange is the new orange.'
jokes.setup: Alexis declines the orange community service vest
jokes.punchline: 'Orange is the new orange' — a deadpan inversion of the Netflix show title that doubles as a practical statement about prison aesthetics
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed one-liner. 'Say yes to the dress' sets up the reality-TV/fashion register, then 'orange is the new orange' subverts the 'black is the new black' fashion idiom AND the show title simultaneously. Tight, quotable, and fully in Ronnie's voice.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: This sweater is a crime against both fashion and basic textile science. The fibers are so poorly constructed, I'm fairly certain they're being held together by nothing more than desperation and a complete lack of self-awareness.

jokes.text: David: 'Who needs 14 grey suits?'
jokes.setup: David is going through his extensive wardrobe trying to decide what to sell
jokes.punchline: The absurdity of owning 14 grey suits is undercut by the fact that David clearly does need all 14
jokes.explanation: A quick visual gag embedded in dialogue. The comedy is in 14 specifically — not 3, not 5, but 14. Efficient character shorthand.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Funky? These are not funky. Funky would be if I wore something with a visible stain, or paired a winter white with an off-white—which, frankly, would be a cry for help. Funky would be if I left my shirt untucked, or worse, wore it backwards. That would be funky. This is curated. This is intentional. This is a aesthetic choice made by someone with taste and restraint.

jokes.text: David: 'What am I supposed to do with my dry clean onlys?!'
jokes.setup: Stevie raises the practical question of David's clothes given their motel situation
jokes.punchline: David's framing of 'dry clean onlys' as a crisis — treating a clothing care instruction as a category of suffering
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'dry clean onlys' (used as a plural noun) is where this lives. David treating it as a legitimate class of endangered beings is excellent character voice.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So you live in a barn?

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: It's a converted barn.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Right, and I'm a converted farm animal.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'I'm still trying to figure out why you'd own more than one piece of clothing you can't put in a washing machine.' David: 'You try putting a cashmere sweater in the washing machine. It'll bite your wrist.'
jokes.setup: Stevie questions the practicality of dry-clean-only clothes
jokes.punchline: 'It'll bite your wrist' — David's cashmere sweater threatening physical violence is a surreal but utterly committed non-sequitur
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's strongest jokes. 'It'll bite your wrist' is genuinely original — it sounds like something David has personally witnessed or perhaps fantasized. The absurdist framing of luxury fabric as sentient and aggressive is perfectly delivered. Stevie's response ('That's a problem I'd like to have') is an ideal button that grounds the absurdity in class contrast without overselling it. High quotability, strong rewatch value.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I got this at Burberry for $800.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I got mine at a gas station for $12.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, that explains a lot.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: What? It's a perfectly good shirt!

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Stevie, it still has a Slim Jim price tag on it.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'It's one chromosome away from a crocheted blanket.'
jokes.setup: Stevie holds up one of David's sweaters and comments on it
jokes.punchline: 'One chromosome away from a crocheted blanket' — a precise, scientific-sounding insult for an article of clothing
jokes.explanation: Exceptionally quotable line. Using 'chromosome' as a unit of aesthetic proximity is genuinely original — it applies the language of biological taxonomy to fashion mockery. The phrase is punchy, perfectly economical, and memorable. It's the kind of line that gets repeated. One of the episode's best-written individual lines.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Why is this so expensive?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Because it's good.

jokes.text: David's extended breakdown over 'funky': 'Yeah, funky is a neon t-shirt you buy at an airport gift shop, next to a bejeweled iPhone case. This... this... is luxury.'
jokes.setup: Stevie keeps using the word 'funky' to describe David's clothes
jokes.punchline: David's deeply wounded, increasingly specific breakdown over being called 'funky' — culminating in a treatise on airport gift shop aesthetics
jokes.explanation: The escalation of David's reaction to a single adjective is excellent cringe comedy. 'Funky' is such a mild word, which makes his mounting outrage funnier with each beat. The airport gift shop reference is inspired specificity — bejeweled iPhone case is exactly the right detail. This sequence shows David's character at its best: genuine wounded pride over something objectively trivial.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Oh, my parents and I get along great. We're very close. My dad's in prison, my mom's in a cult, and my sister's a serial killer, but we text all the time.

jokes.text: David: 'These are my things. I've hand-selected each of these things; they mean a lot to me.' / Then immediately: 'How much do you think I can get for this?'
jokes.setup: David passionately declares his clothes are personally meaningful and irreplaceable
jokes.punchline: Immediately asks what price he can get for one
jokes.explanation: A clean misdirection — the emotional declaration is set up purely to be immediately contradicted. The abruptness of the pivot is the joke. David's unsentimental pragmatism underneath all the performance is a recurring character truth and this is a crisp instance of it.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: I think it's important that I'm honest with you about something. Your father and I, we were not always the most... present parents. We were often preoccupied with our own pursuits, our social calendar, our various business ventures. And if I'm being completely truthful, there were times when I simply forgot you existed.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: But I think that's actually made you stronger. More independent. You had to raise yourself, really, and look how you turned out! Well, I mean, you could be doing better, but given the circumstances, it's quite impressive.

jokes.text: Mutt: 'It was just weed and mushrooms, uppers. Mostly to truckers and my high school teachers.'
jokes.setup: Alexis asks if he was dealing hard drugs
jokes.punchline: 'Mostly to truckers and my high school teachers' — the escalation of clientele from expected (truckers) to darkly funny (teachers) delivered flatly
jokes.explanation: The 'high school teachers' beat is where the laugh is — it subverts the expected list and the deadpan delivery amplifies the dark humor. Solid structure.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: It's simple. You roll the dice, and then you move your piece around the board based on the number you rolled.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, that's Monopoly. We're playing Candyland.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, same concept.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Darling, in Candyland there are no dice. You draw cards from a gumdrop-laden deck.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: And you don't move your piece, you just... go to the place on the card? It's literally a child's game, Dad.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: So you draw cards instead of rolling dice?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Yes.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: And the board is made of candy?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Candy, yes. Licorice trees, lollipop forests—

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: That doesn't make any sense.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It's a game for children, Dad.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: I understand it's for children. I just don't understand how you win if the board is edible.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'It's actually pretty clean around here, which is shocking considering the state of our motel room.'
jokes.setup: Alexis makes a passing comment about the town while picking up garbage
jokes.punchline: The motel room condition as a comparative baseline for 'clean' — a callback to the established squalor
jokes.explanation: A light callback joke. Works as a reminder of the family's circumstances without being too on-the-nose. Functional but not remarkable.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No, no, no. You have to pick two truths and one lie.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I went to Yale. I have a trust fund. And I'm drunk right now.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Okay, so which one is the lie?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The drunk part.

dialogue.speaker: Patrick
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Nothing, if you're a goat.'
jokes.setup: Mutt defensively asks what's wrong with living in a barn
jokes.punchline: Alexis's precise animal comparison — not a horse, not cattle, a goat — delivered with breezy confidence
jokes.explanation: Economically perfect. Four words, clear logic, specific animal choice. 'Goat' is funnier than 'horse' or 'cow' — it's the slightly scrubby, eccentric choice. Alexis's delivery of casual cruelty as simple fact is well-captured.

jokes.text: Mutt: 'It seems to... With the ladies.' / 'That's just a joke.'
jokes.setup: Alexis says the barn 'serves its purpose'
jokes.punchline: Mutt implies it works as a seduction venue, then immediately walks it back — the retraction is as funny as the boast
jokes.explanation: Mild character beat. The comedy of the immediate retraction is more interesting than the brag itself, and it plants the Mutt-Twyla subplot economically.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: It was a joke! I was just joking about the drug lord thing.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: What I meant was, he wasn't a drug lord, he was more of a... drug... distributor.

jokes.text: David: '$850.' Stevie: '850 what?' David: 'Eight hundred—' Stevie: 'Dollars?!' — the ping-pong of shock over David's pants price
jokes.setup: Stevie picks up a pair of pants and asks what they cost
jokes.punchline: Stevie's progressive disbelief — she can't even complete the question before confirming the unit of currency
jokes.explanation: The rapid-fire structure with interrupted lines is well-executed. Stevie's inability to get past '850 what?' before needing to confirm 'Dollars?!' sells the class gap joke efficiently. The rhythm of the exchange is the craft here.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: He's not a drug lord, he's just someone who sells drugs. There's a difference. And honestly, he only got into it because his dad was really into it, so it was kind of a family business thing.

jokes.text: David: 'There's a woman in Paris that makes them by hand.' Stevie: 'My car's worth less than your pants.' David: 'Well, I've seen your car and that makes sense to me.'
jokes.setup: David explains the $850 pants are handmade by a Parisian woman
jokes.punchline: Stevie's car comparison lands; David's immediate agreement with it (while insulting her car) is the button
jokes.explanation: The three-beat structure is clean: emotional appeal → devastating comparison → David agreeing while insulting. David's 'well, I've seen your car' is the ideal punchline button — he takes the insult and weaponizes it simultaneously. Character-specific to both David (unselfconscious elitism) and Stevie (grounded practicality).

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: He's just so... earthy. Like, he doesn't care about hygiene or fashion or any of the things that normal people care about.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Right, well, I mean, some people find that attractive.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Yeah, but he's like, actually dirty. Like, he has dirt under his fingernails and he smells like soil and... he just doesn't shower.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Okay, but that doesn't mean—

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: And you know what? I'm realizing that's actually my type.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, what?

jokes.text: Reaction beat: Alexis alone in the field after Mutt abandons her on community service — she shouts 'Excuse me! What am I supposed to keep doing here?'
jokes.setup: Mutt ditches Alexis mid-community service shift to go see his mother (unknown to Alexis)
jokes.punchline: Alexis left completely alone, shouting into the void — the physical comedy of her abandonment and helplessness
jokes.explanation: A visual/reaction beat that works through physical situation comedy. Alexis's entitled helplessness — shouting after someone who's clearly gone — captures her character well. The 'what am I supposed to keep doing' phrasing shows she's been doing some work, which is funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Twyla
dialogue.line: Wait, so your daughter was kidnapped by a drug lord? That's just like Taken, except Liam Neeson wasn't there.

jokes.text: Twyla: 'I did. It's a little different now. My mom has this thing where half the time she thinks I'm her cousin Angela. And it's getting harder and harder to coordinate visitation times with my dad in prison.'
jokes.setup: Johnny asks Twyla if she gets along with her parents, trying to get advice on parenting
jokes.punchline: Twyla's cheerfully matter-of-fact revelation of her mother's dementia-adjacent confusion and her father's imprisonment — delivered as if this is mildly inconvenient rather than devastating
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's finest jokes and a perfect Twyla moment. The structure is impeccable: Johnny asks an innocent question expecting relatable wisdom, and instead gets two compounding family disasters delivered in the same cheerful register as a coffee order. The 'cousin Angela' detail is inspired — specific enough to be absurd, vague enough to be universally relatable. 'Coordinate visitation times with my dad in prison' is the ideal escalation. Johnny's 'I'm sorry I asked' / Twyla's 'Oh, that's okay!' is a pitch-perfect button. This joke also does character work — Twyla's sunny disposition despite grim circumstances defines her.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Well, have you tried sleeping on your stomach?

jokes.text: Johnny: 'I'm sorry I asked.' Twyla: 'Oh, that's okay! I'll get you your coffees.'
jokes.setup: Twyla has just described her mother's confusion and her imprisoned father
jokes.punchline: Twyla's breezy 'Oh, that's okay!' and immediate pivot to coffee service — treating Johnny's sympathy as a minor social hiccup
jokes.explanation: The button to the Twyla joke that makes the whole sequence sing. Her cheerful pivot is the confirmation that this isn't a cry for help — it's just Twyla being Twyla. The economy is excellent; two short lines do enormous character work.

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Jocelyn, would you like to join us for a bite?

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Oh, I'd love to, but I'm not really in the mood to put anything in my mouth right now.

jokes.text: David: 'You also had company-wide spa days. Why don't we try that?'
jokes.setup: Johnny proposes a family bonding exercise modeled on corporate management retreats
jokes.punchline: David's counter-proposal of spa days — simultaneously revealing his priorities and the absurdity of running a family like a corporation
jokes.explanation: A quick character beat that reveals David's approach to any problem (make it luxurious). Not a standout but efficiently placed.

dialogue.speaker: Wayne
dialogue.line: I'm trying to find the right word for what you're wearing...

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Tasteful?

dialogue.speaker: Wayne
dialogue.line: No...

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Sophisticated?

dialogue.speaker: Wayne
dialogue.line: No...

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, it's 'flamboyant,' isn't it?

jokes.text: The family argument over whether the game is 'two truths and a lie' or 'two lies and a truth' — Johnny keeps getting it wrong
jokes.setup: Johnny proposes a team-building game from his corporate retreats
jokes.punchline: Johnny can't correctly state the rules of a game he's proposing, while David and Alexis can — the irony that the kids are more competent than the parent leading the exercise
jokes.explanation: A sustained bit where the comedy is in Johnny's repeated confident errors and David and Alexis's weary corrections. The escalation of corrections ('no... no... no') works well and the final 'So you've heard of it!' from Johnny is a good button. Solid ensemble piece.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Well, if no one in this family can appreciate a quality sneaker, then I don't know what I'm doing here.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I'm going to my room.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Well, yeah, because babies play that at their birthday parties.'
jokes.setup: Johnny is excited the kids have heard of Two Truths and a Lie
jokes.punchline: Alexis dismisses it as infant-level entertainment
jokes.explanation: A solid zinger. 'Babies play that at their birthday parties' is a good escalation of contempt — not just 'it's for kids' but specifically infant birthday parties. The specificity elevates it.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You know, Mutt, you're like, really attractive, but you're also kind of... homeless-y?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Like, I'm not saying that's a bad thing, it's just very... homeless-y.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You're hot, but homeless-y hot, you know?

jokes.text: David: 'Um, I'm miserable, drunk, and hate this game. So here's a hint... Sadly, I'm not drunk.'
jokes.setup: Johnny asks someone to start Two Truths and a Lie
jokes.punchline: David's three statements are all negative; the lie he reveals is the one that would have helped him cope — 'sadly, I'm not drunk'
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best-written jokes. The structure is inspired: Three statements that are all clearly negative, and David's hint that he's NOT drunk — meaning miserable and hating the game are confirmed truths — is perfectly constructed. 'Sadly, I'm not drunk' is the ideal delivery: the 'sadly' transforms it from a complaint into an elegy. It's highly quotable, deeply character-specific, and the structure uses the game's own rules as the comedic mechanism. This is excellent sitcom writing.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: I know exactly what you're going through. I once dated a guy who was really into role-playing, and let me tell you, the costumes were insane. I mean, I've been in a lot of situations—some of them in public, some of them in cars, some of them in places where I probably shouldn't have been—but the point is, I understand complicated relationships.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'One time I escaped from a Thai drug lord's car trunk by bribing him with sex.'
jokes.setup: Alexis plays Two Truths and a Lie
jokes.punchline: The alleged lie is so extreme it may be true — immediately creates audience uncertainty about Alexis's backstory
jokes.explanation: The joke works on two levels: first as an outrageous game entry, and then as actual character backstory revelation. The ambiguity about whether it's true (it essentially is, as revealed) is the comedic engine. It also sets up the entire Aroon/Thailand subplot that follows. Strong character integration — this particular biography only exists for Alexis.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Jocelyn, we need to talk about what's been going on.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I know. I've been thinking about it too. It's been weighing on me.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: We can't keep doing this. Someone's going to find out.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I know it's wrong, but I can't help myself. I love him so much.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: So do I. He's our son.

jokes.text: Moira: 'Right idea, honey, but you know what? It's gotta be more challenging for everybody.' Johnny: 'Her eyes are aqua.'
jokes.setup: Moira tries to diplomatically redirect Alexis's wild game entry
jokes.punchline: Johnny ignores the diplomacy entirely and just answers the game — 'her eyes are aqua' — revealing he was engaged with the content, not the tone
jokes.explanation: A clean character beat. Moira is in crisis management mode; Johnny is literally playing the game. His delivery of 'her eyes are aqua' as a parenthetical amid the chaos is the joke. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know, I've been thinking about us. About what we have.

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: Oh Roland, I've been thinking about it too.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: He's really grown into quite the man, hasn't he?

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: He really has. I'm so proud of him.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: The way he handles himself, the decisions he makes...

dialogue.speaker: Jocelyn
dialogue.line: I know. And he's so thoughtful now.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: Our son is really something special.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'You did what?!' — delayed reaction to the Thai drug lord revelation
jokes.setup: Alexis says 'I'm kidding' to deflect from her drug lord revelation
jokes.punchline: Johnny's delayed, escalating outrage: 'You did WHAT?!' — he heard it, processed it, and is now catching up to the horror
jokes.explanation: Classic sitcom delayed reaction beat. The delay is the joke — Johnny let the moment pass, then it hit. Works well in a rapid-fire sequence.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: So what, he just doesn't use condoms? That's not an environmental choice, that's just irresponsible.

jokes.text: Alexis defending herself: 'Aroon was a lovely gentleman until he ran out of money.'
jokes.setup: Johnny demands to know what happened in Thailand
jokes.punchline: Alexis's fond, character-reference defense of her drug lord captor — the 'until he ran out of money' rider is the punchline
jokes.explanation: Exceptional line. 'Aroon was a lovely gentleman' spoken as warm reminiscence is already funny; 'until he ran out of money' recontextualizes the entire relationship with ruthless Alexis logic. The economy is outstanding — one clause does all the work. This is deeply character-specific (Alexis's values are entirely transactional), highly quotable, and rewards rewatching because you realize she's essentially describing ALL her relationships this way. One of the episode's finest lines.

dialogue.speaker: Roland
dialogue.line: You know, Mutt's always going on about being eco-friendly and not using plastic. I said to him, 'Son, the only thing you should be worried about not using is a condom.'

jokes.text: Johnny: 'How many people do you know that are currently in the prison system? Is it like twelve?'
jokes.setup: Alexis has just referenced someone named Aroon who presumably ended up incarcerated
jokes.punchline: Johnny's specific number guess — 'like twelve' — implies this is so normalized that a number is the appropriate unit of measure
jokes.explanation: 'Like twelve' is the right number — not three (too reasonable) not a hundred (too absurd) — twelve implies a social circle of criminals that's somehow both specific and believable for Alexis. Good character work for Johnny too, as his concern shifts from outrage to anthropological inquiry.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I created an account under the username 'VintageRoseGoldDealerExtraordaire,' and apparently that's not a legitimate business name.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: The platform flagged it as "suspicious activity."

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Um, I'm pretty sure just two.'
jokes.setup: Johnny estimates Alexis knows about twelve people in prison
jokes.punchline: Alexis corrects him to 'just two' — delivered as if two is a reassuringly low number, with the 'pretty sure' adding uncertainty
jokes.explanation: Beautifully economical. 'I'm pretty sure just two' — the 'pretty sure' implying she might have lost count is the real punchline hiding inside an already-funny line. 'Just two' as a normalizing minimizer is perfect Alexis. Among the episode's most tightly written individual lines.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: These are all natural fibers, hand-stitched by artisans who've devoted their lives to the craft.

dialogue.speaker: Wayne
dialogue.line: That's nice.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: You wouldn't understand. You're probably high right now.

jokes.text: Alexis reveals Mutt is making out with the mayor's wife, and Moira responds: 'What's-her-face?!'
jokes.setup: Alexis casually drops that she saw Mutt making out with the mayor's wife
jokes.punchline: Moira's delighted 'What's-her-face?!' — not horrified, but excited, like she's heard the best gossip of her life
jokes.explanation: Moira's reaction is perfectly calibrated — she doesn't know Jocelyn's name but she knows exactly who this is, and she's thrilled. The 'what's-her-face' construction reveals both her failing to remember the name and her delight simultaneously.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: So you've been sneaking around, meeting up with someone in secret?

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: It's not like that, Alexis.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: You're always disappearing, going off to be alone with this person. What am I supposed to think?

dialogue.speaker: Mutt
dialogue.line: It's just my dad. We're working on the cabin.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh my god.

jokes.text: Moira: 'And the homeless kid? Mm-hmm. Guys like that will bone anything.'
jokes.setup: The family discusses Mutt sleeping with the mayor's wife
jokes.punchline: Moira's blunt, sociological generalization about Mutt's sexual selectivity — delivered as expert analysis
jokes.explanation: Moira's crassness delivered with aristocratic authority is always funny. The 'mm-hmm' confirming it as received wisdom is a good character touch.

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Oh yeah, totally in sync. We all agree that I'm a disappointment, we all agree that I have no skills, we all agree that my career choices were terrible. It's like we're a family that way.

jokes.text: David: 'Well, not anything.' — implication that Mutt's 'anything' excludes him
jokes.setup: Moira says 'guys like that will bone anything'
jokes.punchline: David's 'well, not anything' — implying he himself would be left out — sets up a cascade of horror as Moira starts to follow the logic
jokes.explanation: The funniest sustained bit in the episode. The structure is almost musical: David says 'not anything' → Moira begins to follow the logic → 'that would mean... that would include... you' → the whole family says 'Stop. Stop. Don't. Ew.' in increasingly pained overlapping voices. The joke's genius is in what's NOT said — the ellipsis does the work. The 'Ew.' button from Moira/David/Alexis is perfect and the overlapping 'stop stop stop don't' reads as visual comedy. Pure structure craft.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I have a proposition for you. Something that requires a lot of... commitment. You'd have to come over to my place, clear out some space, and really... go deep.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: I'm talking about organizing my closet.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'I remember when Alexis was just a chubby-faced little girl racing around the house in that banned babywalker, and now she's spending time... In a drug lord's trunk?!'
jokes.setup: Johnny tries to have a nostalgic parenting moment
jokes.punchline: The nostalgic build collapses into 'In a drug lord's trunk?!' — the gap between the image and the reality is the joke
jokes.explanation: The 'banned babywalker' is a lovely character detail (of course the Roses had an illegal baby product) and the nostalgic register makes the punchline land harder. Johnny's disbelief finally surfacing as a coherent sentence is satisfying.

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: I thought the motel was doing well. We had guests last weekend.

dialogue.speaker: Stevie
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, those guests were either running from the law or running towards a very bad life decision. One guy paid in cash and asked if we had a 'no questions asked' policy. Another woman spent three hours in the parking lot just sitting in her car.

jokes.text: Twyla: 'Oh, that is my favourite Liam Neeson movie.'
jokes.setup: Johnny expresses horror that Alexis was held in a drug lord's car trunk
jokes.punchline: Twyla interprets this as a reference to Taken and cheerfully vouches for it — having been eavesdropping and completely misreading the room
jokes.explanation: Perfect Twyla joke. She walks into the middle of a family meltdown and, in complete good faith, recommends Taken. The Liam Neeson callback works because the 'drug lord's trunk' framing genuinely does echo the movie. Her total obliviousness to the real context she's walked into is pitch-perfect. Extremely quotable and one of the best character-integrated jokes in the episode — this joke could not be told by anyone but Twyla.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: My middle name is... Bernard?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Kenneth?

dialogue.speaker: David
dialogue.line: Dad, those are terrible guesses.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Well, I'm running out of ideas here!

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: Maybe it's just something you blocked out?

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Is it... Reginald?

dialogue.speaker: Moira
dialogue.line: Absolutely not.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Why won't anyone just tell me?

jokes.text: Moira upon seeing Jocelyn enter the diner: 'Politician's neglected wife sleeps with a ranch hand. I love it.'
jokes.setup: Moira spots Jocelyn coming into the diner after the gossip revelation
jokes.punchline: Moira's framing of it as a headline / narrative she personally endorses — 'I love it' — from the woman who herself is a neglected wife
jokes.explanation: The joke is enriched by the dramatic irony — Moira as a wealthy, displaced, neglected wife is essentially describing her own latent situation. Her thrill at the gossip is also funny because she's treating real people as characters in her personal drama. 'Ranch hand' vs 'vagrant in a barn' from Johnny is a nice contrast.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Is it... Margaret?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Is it... Beatrice?

dialogue.speaker: Alexis
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Johnny
dialogue.line: Is it... I'm going to need you to tell me, because I am running out of names from your mother's bridge club.

jokes.text: Jocelyn's double entendres about her sore back: 'Even the slightest twitch in bed throws it out.' Moira: 'Perhaps a new position would help?'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn says her lower back is shot and Moira and Johnny know she's been sleeping with Mutt
jokes.punchline: Jocelyn's innocent back complaint sounds like a sexual reference to the audience (and to Moira and Johnny); Moira's 'perhaps a new position would help' is barely disguised
jokes.explanation: Classic double entendre cringe comedy. The audience is ahead of Jocelyn; the Roses are trying to process what they know. Moira's 'new position' is on the line between accidental and intentional, which is the ideal zone for this type of joke.

jokes.text: Jocelyn: 'No, I ate already. Couldn't put another thing in my mouth.' Johnny chuckling awkwardly and clearing his throat.
jokes.setup: Moira invites Jocelyn to join them; Jocelyn declines with an innocent reason
jokes.punchline: 'Couldn't put another thing in my mouth' — the unintentional double entendre lands on Johnny who visibly struggles; his throat-clear is the button
jokes.explanation: The follow-up double entendre in the same scene. Johnny's increasingly pained reaction beats are the real comedy here — Eugene Levy's discomfort is legible even in transcript form. Running the same gag twice risks diminishing returns but the escalation (from 'twitch in bed' to 'couldn't put another thing in my mouth') keeps it fresh.

jokes.text: David to Wayne at the thrift shop: 'David Rose. What are your consignment percentages?'
jokes.setup: David enters a small-town thrift store hoping to sell his luxury clothes
jokes.punchline: David's opener is a formal business inquiry about consignment percentages — treating a junk shop like a high-end boutique
jokes.explanation: Efficient fish-out-of-water character joke. David's formal register — name, consignment inquiry — against a thrift shop backdrop does a lot of work quickly. The reveal that it's just a thrift shop (not a consignment boutique) is the unspoken punchline.

jokes.text: David: 'This is French suede, with a vulcanized-rubber instep, okay? You've lost my trust and my business.'
jokes.setup: Wayne tentatively says he might take the sneakers
jokes.punchline: David's over-specified description of the sneakers (vulcanized-rubber instep) followed by the formal declaration of lost trust and business — over used shoes
jokes.explanation: 'Vulcanized-rubber instep' is a perfectly chosen technical detail — specific enough to sound real, obscure enough to be absurd. 'You've lost my trust and my business' as a formal severance over a thrift shop shoe negotiation is both pompous and perfectly David. High character integration — only David escalates used sneakers to a trust-based business relationship.

jokes.text: Alexis describing Mutt: 'Handsome, mysterious... And handsome and homeless-y'
jokes.setup: Alexis is telling Mutt (to his face) how she thinks he sees himself
jokes.punchline: 'Homeless-y' as an aesthetic category — paired with handsome as if it's a positive modifier, delivered with complete sincerity
jokes.explanation: The coinage 'homeless-y' as a complimentary lifestyle adjective is Alexis at her funniest — she means it well, which makes it worse. The repetition of 'handsome' (three times in the list) suggests she's losing track of her own speech while preoccupied with his looks.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'I have been in the middle of some very messy group scenarios and I know how weird it can be.'
jokes.setup: Alexis tries to tell Mutt she's non-judgmental about his love life
jokes.punchline: 'Very messy group scenarios' — Alexis's euphemistic overshare that says more than she intends
jokes.explanation: Alexis's 'group scenarios' euphemism is a solid character joke — she thinks she's being discreet while being completely explicit. Works well as a character consistency beat.

jokes.text: Alexis: 'Your secret is safe with me. Wink.'
jokes.setup: Alexis promises Mutt she won't tell anyone about his affair with Jocelyn
jokes.punchline: Alexis says the word 'Wink' out loud rather than actually winking — a surreal affectation
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's single most original and quotable line. Alexis saying 'Wink.' as a spoken word rather than performing the action is a perfectly specific, bizarre character tic that tells you everything about how she was raised and how she communicates. It's become one of the show's most referenced Alexis moments. The economy is near-perfect — a single word does enormous comic work. The beat of silence that presumably follows is the visual joke. Highest quotability in the episode.

jokes.text: Roland: 'Doesn't drive in cars, he won't use anything plastic. Good luck with birth control, right?'
jokes.setup: Roland is describing his son Mutt's eccentric lifestyle choices
jokes.punchline: The logical leap from 'won't use anything plastic' to 'good luck with birth control' — Roland connecting his son's environmentalism to his own grandchildren odds
jokes.explanation: Roland's line is a genuinely clever logical extension — the 'no plastic' rule applied to contraception is a lateral comedic leap that feels both surprising and obvious in hindsight. This is Roland at his best: casually vulgar but with actual comedic intelligence underneath. Strong originality for a supporting character.

jokes.text: Jocelyn: 'And then we see you with your kids and you're so... together. What's your secret?' Moira: 'Listening is so very important.'
jokes.setup: Jocelyn admires the Roses as a family unit
jokes.punchline: Moira's 'listening is so very important' — delivered with gravitas by someone who just doesn't know her daughter's middle name and tunes out her children constantly
jokes.explanation: A beautifully ironic callback to the episode's opening theme. Moira dispensing parenting wisdom she demonstrably does not possess — with the full weight of her theatrical self-assurance — is the episode's thematic joke made explicit. Johnny's supporting 'so important to listen... to your kids' sells it further by copying her immediately.

jokes.text: Roland: 'If he'd listened to me, he wouldn't be sitting in a hut, making his own underwear.'
jokes.setup: Roland agrees that listening is important
jokes.punchline: The application of this wisdom to Mutt — the hut and the homemade underwear as evidence of failed listening
jokes.explanation: 'Making his own underwear' as the nadir of poor life choices — not poverty, not unemployment, but specifically homemade underwear — is a perfectly chosen detail. It's eccentric rather than sad, which keeps Roland sympathetic while being funny.

jokes.text: David: 'The Internet is a breeding ground for freaks.' He's been trying to sell sunglasses online and 'dieforfash87' won't pay, claiming they're fake.
jokes.setup: David has moved from thrift store to eBay-style online selling
jokes.punchline: The username 'dieforfash87' not paying because she thinks the sunglasses are fake — a fellow fashion obsessive doubting David's authenticity
jokes.explanation: 'dieforfash87' as a username is a good specific detail — it implies David is operating in the exact world he belongs in (fashion obsessives) and still being rejected. The irony is clean.

jokes.text: David's emotional breakdown about his clothes: 'These clothes are all that I have. Everything else has been taken from me.' Stevie: 'Well, you have your family.' David: 'What's your point?'
jokes.setup: David is catastrophizing about not being able to sell his clothes
jokes.punchline: Stevie offers his family as consolation; David's 'what's your point?' dismisses family as irrelevant to the argument
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best exchanges. 'What's your point?' in response to 'you have your family' is a devastating three-word line that does dual work: it's a great David character joke (his relationship with his family) AND it's structurally perfect — the deflection is so total and rapid that it reveals the depth of the feeling without ever being sentimental. The contrast between David's genuine emotion about his clothes vs. his dismissal of family is the engine of his entire arc. Very high quotability and rewatch value.

jokes.text: David: 'Does it look like I shop on Canal Street?' — in response to his sunglasses being called fake
jokes.setup: dieforfash87 thinks his sunglasses are counterfeit
jokes.punchline: David's rhetorical Canal Street question — invoking the famous counterfeit goods market as the ultimate insult
jokes.explanation: Effective shorthand for David's brand anxiety. Canal Street is the right specific reference — instantly understood in the fashion world as counterfeit territory. Not the strongest joke but economical.

jokes.text: Stevie: 'Wayne huffs keyboard cleaner for a living.' David: 'Well, that's not surprising.'
jokes.setup: Stevie tries to explain why the thrift shop rejected David's clothes
jokes.punchline: Wayne's keyboard cleaner huffing habit as character explanation — and David's immediate, un-surprised acceptance of this information
jokes.explanation: The keyboard cleaner detail is wonderfully specific — not paint thinner, not glue, but keyboard cleaner, which implies a particular type of low-level desperation. David's instant acceptance ('that's not surprising') is both funny and a callback to his Wayne experience.

jokes.text: Stevie offers to store David's clothes in the motel storage area. David: 'Wait, you wanna have sex with me?' Stevie: 'No. I don't think anybody has sex in here unless they're being paid for it.'
jokes.setup: Stevie offers to 'take care of David's stuff' to cheer him up
jokes.punchline: David's interpretation is sexual; Stevie's correction is that the storage room is exclusively a paid sex venue — a double insult
jokes.explanation: A perfectly constructed two-beat joke. David's misinterpretation is in-character (his mind goes there). Stevie's response does two things: corrects him AND implies the room is a known sex work venue, which is its own revelation about the motel. The 'unless they're being paid' clause reframes the entire storage room as a secondary setting with a whole backstory. Excellent economy.

jokes.text: Stevie's catch for storing David's clothes: 'I get to wear whatever I want, anytime I want.'
jokes.setup: Stevie says there's a catch to storing David's clothes
jokes.punchline: The catch is that Stevie gets to wear his designer wardrobe — which David would find catastrophic but reluctantly accepts
jokes.explanation: A satisfying deal-closing joke that also sets up future story. Stevie getting to wear David's clothes is a clever comedic arrangement that works on the class-difference axis the episode has been mining. 'Fine.' as David's response — one word — is the ideal button.

jokes.text: Johnny: 'Our son's clothes are no longer in our closet. Something I said finally got through.' / David: 'I just found somewhere else to put them.' / Johnny: 'Well, subconsciously I got through.'
jokes.setup: Johnny thinks his parental guidance convinced David to move his clothes
jokes.punchline: David corrects him; Johnny immediately reframes his failure as 'subconscious' influence — refusing to accept he had no effect
jokes.explanation: A good callback that resolves the closet conflict while revealing Johnny's self-deception as a parenting mechanism. The 'subconsciously I got through' reframe is a nice character beat — Johnny's optimism is both endearing and delusional.

jokes.text: David: 'We are literally connected by a door that does not lock. How much more connected do you wanna be?'
jokes.setup: Johnny talks about the family being disconnected
jokes.punchline: David's literal interpretation of 'connected' — their physical proximity in the motel as evidence of maximum connection
jokes.explanation: A strong callback to the episode's opening premise. 'A door that does not lock' adds a new detail to the motel's inadequacy, and David's logical literalism ('connected = same room') vs. Johnny's emotional meaning is perfectly David. Well-structured thematic callback.

jokes.text: Moira attempts to reveal Alexis's middle name: 'Emma.' / 'No, Hannah.' / 'Elspeth.' — three wrong guesses in increasing desperation
jokes.setup: The middle name mystery from the episode's opening is revisited as Johnny tries to close the family bonding moment
jokes.punchline: Moira fires off three confidently wrong names — Emma, Hannah, Elspeth — with each guess more certain and more wrong
jokes.explanation: The episode's perfect closing callback. The middle name mystery is the episode's thematic through-line (bad parents don't know their children), and this resolution is both structurally satisfying and funnier than the original setup because: (1) Moira now commits to guesses with equal confidence to her earlier 'of course I do,' (2) the names escalate from generic (Emma, Hannah) to bizarre (Elspeth) suggesting increasing desperation, and (3) Moira's 'I should know!' while being definitively wrong is the ideal button. This is textbook episodic callback construction. Strong rewatch bonus because knowing the joke, you watch Moira's face.

tmdb_overview: Johnny and Moira, feeling like they have failed as parents, try to get to know their grown children. Alexis spies on Mutt and thinks he's having an affair with the Mayor's wife.
insight: David's mortification at his parents' public behavior drives 82-point episode through pure escalation.
